does: This paper introduces ContractBench, a benchmark of 33 dual-axis tasks designed to evaluate whether LLM agents can correctly preserve observation contracts—artifacts like presigned URLs, session tokens, and OAuth parameters whose use is constrained by temporal validity and byte-level integrity. The benchmark systematically probes two failure modes: validity failures (using expired artifacts) and integrity failures (corrupting artifact bytes during processing). Using deterministic evaluation with virtual clocks and SHA-256 hashing for verification, the authors evaluate 38 models and find that no evaluated model achieves 80% accuracy, revealing that contract compliance is an emergent, regression-prone capability not reliably guaranteed by model scale or recency and not consistently improved by general tool-use ability.
connects: This work extends prior research on tool-use and agent reliability in LLMs by focusing on a specific, underexplored failure mode in agentic workflows. It builds on earlier benchmarks measuring LLM tool-use ability and API interaction safety, but identifies that existing evaluations miss critical constraints imposed by external systems on intermediate artifacts. The paper connects to broader work on LLM alignment, safety, and robustness by demonstrating that capability regressions occur even in relatively straightforward tasks, echoing concerns from mechanistic interpretability and adversarial robustness literature about emergent failure modes. It also relates to work on formal verification and contract-based programming, adapting those concepts to measure whether neural agents respect programmatic constraints.
enables: This benchmark enables future research into improving LLM agent reliability for production systems where intermediate API artifacts carry strict constraints. It opens pathways for developing training methods, prompting strategies, or architectural modifications that ensure agents consistently preserve temporal and integrity properties of observations. The work points toward safety verification frameworks for agentic systems, potentially informing formal methods for auditing LLM-based workflows in high-stakes domains. It also suggests directions for studying how models represent and manipulate constrained data, and whether dedicated fine-tuning or in-context learning can address the emergent regression patterns observed across model scales, ultimately supporting deployment of LLM agents in systems with strict operational contracts.
